1	\chapter{The sheaf-of-modules over-equivalence (shared slice root)}
2	\label{chap:Picard_SheafOverEquivalence}
3	% archon:covers AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/SheafOverEquivalence.lean
4	
5	\section{The equivalence}
6	\label{sec:soe_equivalence}
7	
8	\begin{definition}
9	
10	\leanok
11	[Sheaf-of-modules over-equivalence]
12	  \label{def:sheafofmodules_over_equivalence}
13	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.overEquivalence}
14	  % SOURCE: Mathlib code-location provenance (this is an Archon-original assembly
15	  % of existing Mathlib primitives, NOT an external literature citation, so it
16	  % carries no verbatim mathematical SOURCE QUOTE). The primitive instantiated is
17	  % SheafOfModules.pushforwardPushforwardEquivalence, defined at
18	  % Mathlib/Algebra/Category/ModuleCat/Sheaf/PushforwardContinuous.lean:305
19	  % (section Equivalence, lines 289--329): "equivalence of SheafOfModules
20	  % categories from a site equivalence e : C \simeq D together with a morphism of
21	  % the two structure ring sheaves and its inverse, with two coherences". The
22	  % underlying site equivalence is TopologicalSpace.Opens.overEquivalence
23	  % (Mathlib/Topology/Sheaves/Over.lean:41), and the continuity prerequisites are
24	  % the documented TODO at Over.lean:19--22.
25	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and \(U \subseteq X\) an open subset, with associated open
26	  immersion \(\iota : \widetilde{U} \hookrightarrow X\). The reindexing site
27	  equivalence
28	  \[
29	    e \;:=\; \mathtt{Opens.overEquivalence}\,U
30	       \;:\; \mathtt{Over}\,U \;\simeq\; \mathtt{Opens}\,\widetilde{U}
31	  \]
32	  --- between the slice of \(\mathtt{Opens}\,X\) over \(U\) (carrying the slice
33	  topology \((\mathtt{Opens.grothendieckTopology}\,X).\mathtt{over}\,U\)) and the
34	  opens of the subspace \(\widetilde{U}\) (carrying
35	  \(\mathtt{Opens.grothendieckTopology}\,\widetilde{U}\)) --- lifts to an
36	  \emph{equivalence of sheaf-of-modules categories}
37	  \[
38	    \mathtt{overEquivalence}\;:\;
39	      \mathtt{SheafOfModules}\,\bigl((\widetilde{U}).\mathtt{ringCatSheaf}\bigr)
40	        \;\;\simeq\;\;
41	      \mathtt{SheafOfModules}\,\bigl(X.\mathtt{ringCatSheaf}.\mathtt{over}\,U\bigr),
42	  \]
43	  the modules-level lift of \(e\) that transports the structure ring sheaf. It is
44	  obtained by instantiating
45	  \(\mathtt{SheafOfModules.pushforwardPushforwardEquivalence}\) at \(e\), with the
46	  open-immersion structure-sheaf ring morphism
47	  \[
48	    \varphi \;:\;
49	      X.\mathtt{ringCatSheaf}.\mathtt{over}\,U
50	      \;\longrightarrow\;
51	      \bigl(e.\mathtt{functor}.\mathtt{sheafPushforwardContinuous}\bigr).\mathrm{obj}\,
52	        \bigl((\widetilde{U}).\mathtt{ringCatSheaf}\bigr)
53	  \]
54	  --- sectionwise at an object \(V \in \mathtt{Over}\,U\) the open-immersion
55	  comparison \(\mathcal{O}_X(V.\mathrm{left}) \to \mathcal{O}_{\widetilde{U}}(e\,V)\),
56	  i.e.\ the section-level isomorphism \(\iota.\mathtt{appIso}\) of the structure
57	  sheaves --- together with its inverse \(\psi\) and the two coherences
58	  \(H_1, H_2\) expressing that \(\varphi\) and \(\psi\) are mutually inverse.
59	\end{definition}
60	
61	\begin{proof}
62	
63	\leanok
64	  The construction is the assembly of three existing Mathlib primitives; the only
65	  step carrying mathematical content is the ring morphism \(\varphi\).
66	
67	  \emph{Continuity (no work).} The equivalence
68	  \(\mathtt{pushforwardPushforwardEquivalence}\) requires both legs of the site
69	  equivalence \(e\) to be continuous:
70	  \(e.\mathtt{functor}\) and \(e.\mathtt{inverse}\). For a dense-subsite inclusion
71	  continuity is automatic --- it is a typeclass instance --- and for an
72	  equivalence the density of one leg propagates to the other. The declaration
73	  \(\mathtt{overEquivInverseIsDenseSubsite}\) exhibits \(e.\mathtt{inverse}\) as a
74	  dense subsite of the slice site, from which both continuity legs follow by
75	  inference. The Mathlib TODO at \texttt{Topology/Sheaves/Over.lean} (lines 19--22)
76	  is discharged for this case.
77	
78	  \emph{The ring morphism \(\varphi\) (the genuine content).} The two structure
79	  ring sheaves are different objects on different sites:
80	  \(X.\mathtt{ringCatSheaf}.\mathtt{over}\,U\) over \(\mathtt{Over}\,U\), and
81	  \((\widetilde{U}).\mathtt{ringCatSheaf}\) over \(\mathtt{Opens}\,\widetilde{U}\).
82	  The open immersion \(\iota\) identifies them: at an object \(V \in
83	  \mathtt{Over}\,U\) the sections \(\mathcal{O}_X(V.\mathrm{left})\) and
84	  \(\mathcal{O}_{\widetilde{U}}(\iota^{-1}(V.\mathrm{left}))\) agree through the
85	  open-immersion section isomorphism \(\iota.\mathtt{appIso}\). This is verbatim the
86	  datum that \(\mathtt{Scheme.Modules.restrictFunctor}\) already uses inline
87	  (its structure map is \((\iota.\mathtt{appIso}\,\cdot).\mathrm{inv}\)). Packaging
88	  this section-level isomorphism naturally in \(V\) yields \(\varphi\); its inverse
89	  is \(\psi\), and the coherences \(H_1, H_2\) record that the
90	  \(\iota.\mathtt{appIso}\) round-trips are identities, proved by the
91	  inverse-iso laws (\(\mathtt{Sheaf.hom\_ext}\) reduces them sectionwise).
92	
93	  Feeding \(e\), \(\varphi\), \(\psi\), \(H_1\), \(H_2\) into
94	  \(\mathtt{pushforwardPushforwardEquivalence}\) produces the asserted equivalence.
95	  Its underlying functor is \(\mathtt{SheafOfModules.pushforward}\,\varphi\), which
96	  is what the two consumer isomorphisms below unfold.
97	\end{proof}
98	
99	\section{The equivalence carries restriction and unit}
100	\label{sec:soe_consumers}
101	
102	\begin{lemma}
103	
104	\leanok
105	[The over-equivalence carries restriction to over]
106	  \label{lem:sheafofmodules_restrict_over_iso}
107	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.restrictOverIso}
108	  \uses{def:sheafofmodules_over_equivalence}
109	  Let \(M \in \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\). Under the over-equivalence of
110	  \cref{def:sheafofmodules_over_equivalence} there is a canonical isomorphism
111	  \[
112	    \mathtt{overEquivalence}.\mathtt{functor}.\mathrm{obj}\,\bigl(M|_\iota\bigr)
113	      \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\;
114	    M.\mathtt{over}\,U,
115	  \]
116	  i.e.\ the equivalence carries the restriction \(M|_\iota\) of \(M\) along the
117	  open immersion to the slice object \(M.\mathtt{over}\,U\).
118	\end{lemma}
119	
120	\begin{proof}
121	  \uses{def:sheafofmodules_over_equivalence}
122	  \leanok
123	  The restriction \(M|_\iota\) is \emph{itself} a pushforward of sheaves of
124	  modules: by construction \(M|_\iota = \mathtt{SheafOfModules.pushforward}\)
125	  applied along \(\iota.\mathtt{opensFunctor}\) (the open-immersion functor
126	  \(\mathtt{Opens}\,\widetilde{U} \to \mathtt{Opens}\,X\)). The functor underlying
127	  the over-equivalence is \(\mathtt{SheafOfModules.pushforward}\,\varphi\). Their
128	  composite is again a single pushforward, identified by
129	  \(\mathtt{SheafOfModules.pushforwardComp}\), which here is the identity
130	  isomorphism \(\mathtt{Iso.refl}\). It remains to identify the two underlying
131	  index functors \(\mathtt{Over}\,U \to \mathtt{Opens}\,X\) entering the two
132	  pushforwards: both send an object \(V\) to its left component \(V.\mathrm{left}\),
133	  so they are equal, and \(\mathtt{pushforwardNatIso}\) along the \(\mathtt{eqToIso}\)
134	  of that equality transports the composite to \(M.\mathtt{over}\,U\). The whole
135	  argument mirrors, step for step, the existing
136	  \(\mathtt{Scheme.Modules.restrictFunctorAdjCounitIso}\).
137	\end{proof}
138	
139	\begin{lemma}
140	
141	\leanok
142	[The over-equivalence carries unit to unit]
143	  \label{lem:sheafofmodules_unit_over_iso}
144	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.unitOverIso}
145	  \uses{def:sheafofmodules_over_equivalence}
146	  Under the over-equivalence of \cref{def:sheafofmodules_over_equivalence} there is
147	  a canonical isomorphism
148	  \[
149	    \mathtt{overEquivalence}.\mathtt{functor}.\mathrm{obj}\,
150	      \bigl(\mathtt{SheafOfModules.unit}\,(\widetilde{U}).\mathtt{ringCatSheaf}\bigr)
151	      \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\;
152	    \mathtt{SheafOfModules.unit}\,\bigl(X.\mathtt{ringCatSheaf}.\mathtt{over}\,U\bigr),
153	  \]
154	  i.e.\ the equivalence carries the unit module (the structure sheaf-as-module) of
155	  the subspace site to the unit module of the slice site.
156	\end{lemma}
157	
158	\begin{proof}
159	  \uses{def:sheafofmodules_over_equivalence}
160	  \leanok
161	  The functor underlying the over-equivalence is
162	  \(\mathtt{SheafOfModules.pushforward}\,\varphi\). The pushforward of a unit module
163	  along a morphism of ringed sites is the unit module of the target ring sheaf,
164	  transported along the ring identification \(\varphi\): the structure sheaf
165	  pushes forward to the structure sheaf because \(\varphi\) is precisely the
166	  open-immersion identification of the two structure ring sheaves. Concretely the
167	  pushforward-of-unit computation produces \(\mathtt{unit}\) of the codomain ring
168	  sheaf up to \(\varphi\), and \(\varphi\) being an isomorphism makes this a
169	  genuine isomorphism.
170	
171	  Concretely, the canonical comparison map realising this transport is the
172	  unit-to-pushforward-of-unit morphism
173	  \[
174	    \mathtt{SheafOfModules.unitToPushforwardObjUnit}\,\varphi
175	      \;:\;
176	      \mathtt{unit}\,\bigl(X.\mathtt{ringCatSheaf}.\mathtt{over}\,U\bigr)
177	      \;\longrightarrow\;
178	      (\mathtt{pushforward}\,\varphi).\mathrm{obj}\,
179	        \bigl(\mathtt{unit}\,(\widetilde{U}).\mathtt{ringCatSheaf}\bigr).
180	  \]
181	  Its section at an open \(W\) is exactly the section-level ring map
182	  \(\varphi.\mathrm{app}\,W\): the morphism acts on a section by multiplication
183	  through \(\varphi\), so on \(W\) it is the additive-group map underlying
184	  \(\varphi.\mathrm{app}\,W\) (the characterisation
185	  \(\mathtt{unitToPushforwardObjUnit\_val\_app\_apply}\)). Since \(\varphi\) is an
186	  isomorphism of ring sheaves, each component \(\varphi.\mathrm{app}\,W\) is a ring
187	  isomorphism, hence its underlying additive-group map is an isomorphism.
188	
189	  It follows that \(\mathtt{unitToPushforwardObjUnit}\,\varphi\) is an isomorphism.
190	  One reflects the sectionwise isomorphism upward through the two forgetful
191	  functors --- first \(\mathtt{SheafOfModules.forget}\) (down to presheaves of
192	  modules), then \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.toPresheaf}\) (down to presheaves of
193	  additive groups) --- so that being an isomorphism reduces to the componentwise
194	  isomorphism check
195	  \(\mathtt{NatTrans.isIso\_iff\_isIso\_app}\) on the underlying natural
196	  transformation, which is the sectionwise statement just established. The desired
197	  isomorphism \(\mathtt{unitOverIso}\) is then the inverse of the resulting
198	  isomorphism, \((\mathtt{asIso}\,(\mathtt{unitToPushforwardObjUnit}\,\varphi))^{-1}\).
199	  This is the slice-site analogue of
200	  \(\mathtt{pullbackObjUnitToUnitIso}\).
201	\end{proof}
202	
203	\section{The engine corollary}
204	\label{sec:soe_chart}
205	
206	\begin{lemma}
207	
208	\leanok
209	[Over--restrict trivialisation bridge]
210	  \label{lem:chart_over_iso}
211	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.chartOverIso}
212	  \uses{lem:sheafofmodules_restrict_over_iso, lem:sheafofmodules_unit_over_iso}
213	  Let \(M \in \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) and \(U \subseteq X\) open, and suppose
214	  given a trivialisation of the restriction along the open immersion
215	  \(\iota : \widetilde{U} \hookrightarrow X\),
216	  \[
217	    e \;:\; M|_\iota \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\;
218	      \mathtt{SheafOfModules.unit}\,(\widetilde{U}).\mathtt{ringCatSheaf}.
219	  \]
220	  Then there is a canonical isomorphism in the slice category
221	  \[
222	    \mathtt{chartOverIso}\,M\,U\,e \;:\;
223	      M.\mathtt{over}\,U \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\;
224	      \mathtt{SheafOfModules.unit}\,\bigl(X.\mathtt{ringCatSheaf}.\mathtt{over}\,U\bigr).
225	  \]
226	\end{lemma}
227	
228	\begin{proof}
229	  \uses{lem:sheafofmodules_restrict_over_iso, lem:sheafofmodules_unit_over_iso,
230	        def:sheafofmodules_over_equivalence}
231	        \leanok
232	  Transport the trivialisation \(e\) across the over-equivalence and reconcile the
233	  two ends with the consumer isomorphisms. Explicitly, \(\mathtt{chartOverIso}\,M\,U\,e\)
234	  is the composite
235	  \[
236	    M.\mathtt{over}\,U
237	      \xrightarrow[\sim]{\ (\mathtt{restrictOverIso})^{-1}\ }
238	      \mathtt{overEquivalence}.\mathtt{functor}.\mathrm{obj}\,\bigl(M|_\iota\bigr)
239	      \xrightarrow[\sim]{\ \mathtt{overEquivalence}.\mathtt{functor}.\mathtt{mapIso}\,e\ }
240	      \mathtt{overEquivalence}.\mathtt{functor}.\mathrm{obj}\,(\mathtt{unit}\,\cdots)
241	      \xrightarrow[\sim]{\ \mathtt{unitOverIso}\ }
242	      \mathtt{unit}\,\bigl(X.\mathtt{ringCatSheaf}.\mathtt{over}\,U\bigr).
243	  \]
244	  The first arrow is the inverse of \cref{lem:sheafofmodules_restrict_over_iso}; the
245	  middle arrow is the image of \(e\) under the (functor of the) over-equivalence,
246	  which is functorial in isomorphisms hence sends the iso \(e\) to an iso; the last
247	  arrow is \cref{lem:sheafofmodules_unit_over_iso}. Each factor is an isomorphism,
248	  so the composite is.
249	
250	  This lemma addresses the site mismatch in the chart-presentation of
251	  \cref{lem:lbc_chart_presentation}: the given trivialisation \(e\) lives in
252	  \(\mathtt{SheafOfModules}\,(\widetilde{U}).\mathtt{ringCatSheaf}\) (the open
253	  subspace site), whereas the finite-presentation transport
254	  \(\mathtt{Presentation.ofIsIso}\) must act in
255	  \(\mathtt{SheafOfModules}\,(X.\mathtt{ringCatSheaf}.\mathtt{over}\,U)\) (the slice
256	  site); \(\mathtt{chartOverIso}\) crosses that gap. It is stated in full
257	  generality in \(M\), \(U\), \(e\) for use in
258	  \cref{chap:Picard_LineBundleCoherence}. The over-equivalence of
259	  \cref{def:sheafofmodules_over_equivalence} likewise underlies the internal-Hom
260	  theory: the commutativity of the internal Hom with the slice-site change of base
261	  (\cref{lem:dual_restrict_iso}) passes through the same equivalence.
262	\end{proof}

67

69	
70	The plan agent has decided that a specific blueprint chapter (`blueprint/src/chapters/<slug>.tex`) needs to change — to reflect a strategic decision, fill a missing definition or theorem the project needs, or align the informal prose with the current Lean structure. Your directive tells you which chapter, what must be there, and what counts as out-of-scope.

You do NOT edit other chapters, `.lean` files, or `content.tex`. Cross-chapter inconsistencies you spot go in the report's "Notes for Plan Agent" section — you flag them, you don't fix them.
73

75	
76	```markdown
77	# Blueprint Writer Directive
78	
79	## Slug
80	<slug>

107	## Chapter format
108	
109	Each declaration block in a chapter looks like:
110	
111	```latex
112	\begin{theorem}[name_for_humans]
113	  \label{thm:some_label}
114	  \lean{namespace.theorem_name}
115	  \uses{def:related_definition, lem:supporting_lemma}
116	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], III.5.1, p. 174  (read from references/hartshorne-III-5.md)
117	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "A morphism $f: X \to Y$ of schemes locally of finite
118	  % type is said to be smooth at $x \in X$ if there exist an open affine
119	  % neighborhood $V = \Spec B$ of $f(x)$ and an open affine neighborhood
120	  % $U = \Spec A$ of $x$ with $f(U) \subset V$ such that ..."
121	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, III.5.1.}
122	  Informal statement of the theorem, in the project's notation.
123	\end{theorem}
124	
125	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "Proof. We may assume $Y = \Spec B$ and
126	% $X = \Spec A$ are affine. Then $f$ corresponds to a ring homomorphism
127	% $\varphi: B \to A$, and $f$ is smooth at $x$ if and only if ..."
128	\begin{proof}
129	  \uses{thm:another_result}
130	  Step-by-step informal proof, in the project's notation. Reference blueprint labels
131	  with \uses{...} so the dependency graph stays accurate.
132	\end{proof}
133	```
134	
135	Use `\definition`, `\lemma`, `\theorem`, `\proposition`, `\corollary` as appropriate. `\lean{...}` names the Lean declaration this block corresponds to. `\uses{...}` records cross-references; keep it accurate so `leanblueprint`'s dependency graph remains usable.
136	
137	### Citation discipline (the hard rule)
138	
139	**Every declaration block that derives from external reference material requires three citation elements:**
140	
141	1. **`% SOURCE:` LaTeX comment** — pointer + local file. Format: `% SOURCE: <citation pointer> (read from references/<file>.md)`. The pointer is the source identifier + section/theorem/definition number + page when available (e.g. `[Hartshorne], III.5.1, p. 174` or `[Stacks Project], Tag 01V4`). The `(read from references/<file>.md)` parenthetical names the local file you opened to produce the verbatim quote.
142	2. **`% SOURCE QUOTE:` LaTeX comment** — the **verbatim text** of the cited statement, copied character-by-character from the local reference file you just named in the `% SOURCE:` line. Verbatim means:
143	   - **Original language.** French for Bourbaki / EGA, German for Grothendieck's early notes, English for Hartshorne / Vakil / Stacks, etc. Do NOT translate.
144	   - **Original notation preserved.** If the source writes $\mathcal{O}_X^*$ and the project uses $\mathcal{O}_X^\times$, the verbatim quote keeps $\mathcal{O}_X^*$. The project-notation restatement happens in the prose body that follows, NOT in the quote.
145	   - **Every word, every symbol.** No paraphrase, no "for brevity" abbreviation. The quote is the anti-hallucination signal — a writer who actually opened the source can paste; a writer reconstructing from memory cannot reproduce the source's exact words.
146	3. **Visible `\textit{Source: <pointer>.}` line** — first line of the prose body. Renders in the PDF so a mathematician reading the typeset blueprint sees the citation at a glance.
147	
148	**For proof blocks**: include a `% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:` LaTeX comment **immediately before** the `\begin{proof}` environment (NOT inside it). Same verbatim rules — original language, original notation, every word. The informal proof body inside `\begin{proof}...\end{proof}` is the project's restated version in project notation, what the prover formalizes.
149	
150	When a source proof is too long for one verbatim block (multi-page construction): split the theorem into sub-lemmas in the directive's logical structure, and give each sub-statement its own `% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:`. One opaque mega-quote defeats verifiability. If even sub-splitting is impractical, report it in "Notes for Plan Agent" — do not silently drop the verbatim quote.
151	
152	**For Archon-original / project-bespoke results** (the directive does not name an external source for this block): the source lines are omitted — the block stands on the proof sketch alone.
153	
154	**The hard rule, explicit:**
155	
156	> **You may NEVER write `% SOURCE:`, `% SOURCE QUOTE:`, `% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:`, or `\textit{Source: ...}` from training memory.** Every such line must be backed by a local file under `references/` that you have **opened and read in this session**, and the verbatim quote must be a character-by-character copy of text from that file.
157	
158	The `(read from references/<file>.md)` parenthetical in `% SOURCE:` is your discipline check. If, when reviewing your own draft, you find a block where you cannot point to the specific local file you read to produce the quote — you fabricated it. Delete the quote. Either dispatch a `reference-retriever` (see below) to obtain the missing source, or report the block as INCOMPLETE under "Notes for Plan Agent".
159	
160	If the directive named a source but the local reference file is missing or doesn't contain the specific statement you need:
161	
162	- **Dispatch a `reference-retriever` mid-session** (see "Dispatching a reference-retriever" below) to fetch the missing material into `references/<slug>.md`.
163	- **Wait for the retriever to return**, then **open and read the new file**.
164	- THEN write the citation block, citing the new local file in the `% SOURCE:` parenthetical.
165	
166	If retrieval fails (paywall, broken link, no API key, not available online): mark the block `% SOURCE: <pointer> (verbatim text not yet retrieved)` and skip it. Report it as INCOMPLETE. Do NOT substitute a paraphrase, a recollection, or a translation as the verbatim quote — that's the iter-149 failure mode and the entire rule exists to prevent it.
167	
168	## Rules
169	
170	### What you CAN do
171	- Add new declaration blocks (definitions, lemmas, theorems, propositions, corollaries) under direction.
172	- Expand or revise existing prose / proof sketches in your assigned chapter.
173	- **Add, correct, or complete `\uses{...}` cross-references** so the dependency edges match the real mathematics. If your chapter's proof of `T` invokes lemma `L`, `T` must declare `\uses{lem:L}`; if a `\uses{}` points at a label that no longer exists, fix it. Verify with `leandag` (see "Verifying dependencies with leandag" below) — do not eyeball it.
174	- Adjust `\lean{...}` hints when the directive names a new Lean target.
175	- **Write entries for Lean helper declarations.** The project keeps a 1-to-1 Lean ↔ blueprint correspondence: when the directive lists a helper that exists only in Lean (a prover-created lemma, an instance, a decomposition step), give it a real entry — concise informal statement, `\label{}`, `\lean{}` with the exact Lean name, accurate `\uses{}`, and at least a one-line informal proof. "It's trivial" is not a reason to skip it: the entry is what carries the dependency edges and what helps a later prover fill the sorry. When the helper's Lean proof relies on a fact with no blueprint entry, flag it in "Notes for Plan Agent" (or create it too, if it belongs in your chapter).
176	- **Author a *Mathlib dependency anchor* and mark it `\mathlibok`** when your chapter relies on a result Mathlib already provides — write out the statement and point `\lean{}` at the real Mathlib declaration (see "Mathlib dependency anchors" below). This is the only marker you may add.
177	- **Remove a declaration block ONLY when the directive explicitly lists it for removal** (typically an isolated/orphaned statement the reviewer flagged `remove`). Default to wiring an isolated node into the graph (add the missing `\uses{}`), not deleting it — an isolated node is usually a missing edge, not dead weight. Never remove the project goal, and never remove a block whose `\lean{}` still names a live Lean declaration.
178	- Read `references/summary.md` and any reference that is in `references/` to ground your writing in the project's sources.
179	
180	### What you MUST do
181	- **Keep the chapter valid LaTeX.** Don't leave dangling `\begin{...}` without matching `\end{...}`. Compile-checking is the plan agent's responsibility but you must not introduce syntax errors.
182	- **Stay within your chapter.** Your declared write-domain is one `*.tex` file. The Archon CLI rejects writes outside it.
183	- **Define non-standard macros in `blueprint/src/macros/common.tex`** before using them — but: that file is outside your write-domain, so you DON'T touch it. If the directive requires a new macro, you note in your report "needs macro `\foo`" and leave the LaTeX using the new command name; the plan agent adds the macro before next iter's typeset.
184	- **Use mathematical, not Lean-syntactic prose.** Describe the proof in the language of mathematics — definitions, set inclusions, ring maps, universal properties — not in Lean tactic syntax. The prover formalizes your math.
185	- **Follow citation discipline** for every block derived from external reference material (see "Citation discipline (the hard rule)" above). Each such block needs `% SOURCE:` with a local-file parenthetical, `% SOURCE QUOTE:` with a verbatim original-language quote, `% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:` before the proof env when applicable, and a visible `\textit{Source: ...}` prefix. Never cite from memory — only from a local `references/<file>.md` you opened and read in this session.
186	- **Document every change** in your report, including which `references/<file>.md` files you opened (under "References consulted").
187	
188	### What you MUST NOT do
189	- **Do NOT reference project history.** No "since iteration N," "after our failed attempt," or session narrative. The blueprint must read as a standalone mathematical document.
190	- **Do NOT include Lean implementation details.** No tactic suggestions, typeclass wiring notes, or Lean code structure hints in the prose. If a Lean note is needed, it belongs in `.lean` files (the plan agent will use `lean-scaffolder` for that).
191	- **Do NOT add `\leanok` markers.** `\leanok` is earned by a sorry-free Lean proof and set by the deterministic `sync_leanok` phase — never by you. (`\mathlibok` is different: you MAY add it, but ONLY on a Mathlib dependency anchor — see "Mathlib dependency anchors" below — never on the project's own to-be-proved declarations.)
192	- **Do NOT edit other chapters.** Even when you spot a related issue, flag it in "Notes for Plan Agent" instead of fixing it.
193	- **Do NOT edit `content.tex`** (the top-level blueprint file that `\input`s the chapters).
194	- **Do NOT edit `.lean` files** or any other state file.
195	- **Do NOT write Lean syntax** — keep the chapter mathematical, not syntactic.
196	- **Do NOT write literal `REF` placeholders.** Never produce prose like "Definition~REF" or "Sections REF–REF". Cross-reference declarations with `\cref{<label>}` using the real label (look it up in the chapter or via `leandag`); when the target genuinely has no label yet, describe it by name in prose. The blueprint-doctor flags every literal `REF` as a malformed reference.
197	- **Do NOT interleave math delimiters.** Pick `\( … \)` for a formula and close it with `\)` — never produce `$ … \( … \) … $` or `X\(, hence \)Y` inversions; the doctor's `math-delim` lint flags every such site and the rendered output shreds mid-formula.
198	- **Do NOT paste bare label ids into prose.** "Thm.~th:main" is not a reference — write `\cref{...}` for project declarations, or the source's human-readable number ("Thm.~4.8") when quoting external material.
199	- **Do NOT use undefined macros.** Every `\foo` your prose needs must exist in `blueprint/src/macros/common.tex` or be `\providecommand`'d at the top of your chapter (chapter-local is fine; report a "needs macro" note for shared ones).
200	- **Do NOT expand scope.** Stick to what the directive listed under "Required content".
201	- **Do NOT fabricate citations.** Never write `% SOURCE:`, `% SOURCE QUOTE:`, `% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:`, or `\textit{Source: ...}` from memory. If you don't have a local `references/<file>.md` containing the source text, dispatch a retriever and wait. The `(read from references/<file>.md)` parenthetical is the discipline check — if you cannot truthfully point to the file you read, the citation block is not allowed in the chapter.
202	- **Do NOT translate or restate the verbatim quote.** `% SOURCE QUOTE:` and `% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:` contain the source's original language, original notation, every word as-is. Project-notation rewrites belong in the rendered prose body, not in the verbatim comment.

204	## Mathlib dependency anchors (`\mathlibok`)
205	
206	When your chapter's proofs rely on a definition or lemma that **Mathlib already provides** (and the project will not re-prove), it is good practice — not just a bare `\uses{}` to nowhere — to make that dependency a first-class blueprint block: a **Mathlib dependency anchor**. Doing so:
207	
208	- makes the reliance explicit and readable (a mathematician sees exactly which Mathlib result the route stands on);
209	- gives `leandag` a real node, so the `\uses{}` resolves (no broken/unknown ref) and the node's effort is **done** rather than ∞;
210	- keeps it clear how much the route leans on Mathlib vs. on original work.
211	
212	Write the anchor like any statement block, with three differences:
213	
214	```latex
215	\begin{lemma}[Hensel's lemma]
216	  \label{lem:hensel_mathlib}
217	  \lean{Mathlib.RingTheory.Henselian.foo}   % the REAL Mathlib declaration
218	  \mathlibok
219	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
220	  Statement of the result in the project's notation.
221	\end{lemma}
222	```
223	
224	- **`\lean{}` names the actual Mathlib declaration** (with its real namespace), so `leandag` matches the node to Mathlib.
225	- **`\mathlibok`** marks it as supplied by Mathlib — the DAG treats it as done; no `\begin{proof}` is needed.
226	- No `% SOURCE:`/`% SOURCE QUOTE:` block — Mathlib is the source; the `\lean{}` target *is* the citation.
227	
228	**The anti-hallucination rule (mirrors citation discipline):** mark `\mathlibok` ONLY when the statement genuinely exists in Mathlib and your stated form is faithful to it. A wrong or invented `\mathlibok` is worse than an ∞ hole — the loop will treat a non-existent result as available and skip proving a real gap. So:
229	
230	- Prefer anchors the **directive names** (the plan agent/reviewer identified them as Mathlib results).
231	- If you are not confident the exact statement is in Mathlib, do **not** mark `\mathlibok` — write the block without it (or leave the `\uses{}` for the reviewer to resolve) and flag it under "Notes for Plan Agent" so the reviewer can verify against Lean.
232	- Never put `\mathlibok` on one of the project's **own** to-be-proved declarations. That marker is for Mathlib-supplied results only; `\leanok` (project proofs) is never yours to add.
233

235	
236	You write mathematics, not literature criticism — your prose must be grounded in authoritative sources, not your training memory. Before composing any new declaration block:
237	
238	1. **Read `references/summary.md`** to see the index of sources the project already has. Sources directly relevant to your chapter are your first stop.
239	2. **Read every reference file your directive names** under its `## References` section. The plan agent named those because the chapter needs them.
240	3. **Read anything in `references/summary.md` your directive didn't name but that is clearly relevant** to your chapter (same area, same theorem, same construction).
241	
242	Do NOT write content from your training memory when you could ground it in a reference instead. When the references say one thing and your memory says another, trust the references.

244	## Dispatching a reference-retriever (when the project's sources don't have what you need)
245	
246	If, while drafting, you discover that the chapter needs material the project's `references/` doesn't contain, **dispatch a `reference-retriever` mid-session** rather than guessing or papering over the gap.
247	
248	Conditions for dispatch:
249	
250	- The directive named a source (`Smith 2018`, an arXiv ID) that isn't in `references/` yet.
251	- The required content is in a known textbook (Hartshorne, Vakil, Stacks Project, etc.) but the project hasn't summarized the relevant chapter.
252	- You need a Mathlib-adjacent source (nLab, math overflow, Stacks Project) the directive didn't anticipate.
253	
254	Dispatch (Bash; treat as blocking and await the report; in your write-domain only if it includes `references/**`):
255	
256	```
257	python3 .claude/tools/archon-subagent.py \
258	  --name reference-retriever \
259	  --slug <kebab-slug-for-the-source> \
260	  --directive-file .archon/logs/iter-NNN/<your-slug>/reference-retriever-<child-slug>-directive.md \
261	  --write-domain 'references/**'
262	```
263	
264	Write the directive file first; the directive format is documented in `.archon/subagents/reference-retriever.md`. The retriever returns when the new summary is on disk; **then resume writing**, citing the new reference.

266	If your invocation's recorded write-domain does NOT include `references/**` (your `parent.write_domain` in `dispatch.jsonl`), the dispatch will be rejected. In that case, STOP writing the affected section, report the missing source in "Notes for Plan Agent", and finish the parts of the chapter you can still write. The plan agent will re-dispatch you next iter with the broader write-domain.

269	
270	1. Read your directive completely.
271	2. **Read `references/summary.md`** and every reference your directive points at; also read sibling-chapter material that informs cross-references. Track which local files you actually opened — you will need their paths for the `% SOURCE:` parenthetical.
272	3. Read the target chapter currently on disk to see what's already there.
273	4. Plan the edits: which blocks to add, which to revise. Decide where each new block goes in the chapter's existing flow. For each block that derives from an external source, identify the specific local `references/<file>.md` containing the verbatim statement (and proof, if applicable). If no local file covers the statement, list it as "retrieval needed" for the next step.
274	5. **For every "retrieval needed" item, dispatch a `reference-retriever`** (see above) and **wait** for it to return. THEN open and read the newly-written `references/<slug>.md` before drafting the citing block. Do not draft citation blocks against pending or imagined sources.
275	6. Make the edits. For each citation block: copy `% SOURCE QUOTE:` and `% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:` content character-by-character from the local reference file — original language, original notation, every word. Do not transcribe from a window of the file you "remember reading"; have the file open and copy.

277	8. **Verify dependencies with `leandag`** (see the section below): confirm your chapter introduced no broken `\uses{}` and left none of your blocks unintentionally isolated. Fix the edges and re-check.
278	9. Write your report. List under "References consulted" every local file you opened in step 2 and step 5.

280	## Verifying dependencies with leandag
281	
282	`leandag` parses the real Lean ↔ blueprint dependency DAG, so use it to check your `\uses{}` instead of trusting your memory of the graph. It is read-only on the blueprint (it never edits your chapter); run it after you make edits:
283	
284	```
285	leandag build --json                       # report: unknown_uses, unmatched_lean, isolated count
286	leandag query --isolated --chapter <c>     # isolated nodes in your chapter (no edges in or out)
287	leandag show isolated                       # all isolated nodes, project-wide
288	```
289	
290	`leandag` is on the same PATH as `archon`; it is the one tool you use to query the DAG.
291	
292	What to do with the output, scoped to **your chapter**:
293	
294	- **`unknown_uses` naming a label in your chapter** → you wrote a `\uses{}` pointing at a label that doesn't exist. Fix the label, or — if the target genuinely belongs in your chapter and the directive calls for it — add the missing declaration. If the missing label lives in another chapter, leave a correct `\uses{}` to it and note it under "Notes for Plan Agent" (you don't edit that chapter).
295	- **A block you wrote shows up as isolated** → it has no `\uses{}` out and nothing uses it. This is almost always a *missing edge*: add the `\uses{}` that ties it to the declarations its statement/proof actually depends on (and check whether a sibling result should `\uses{}` it). Only treat an isolated block as removable when your directive explicitly authorized removing it.
296	- **Removal** (directive-authorized only): delete the orphaned block cleanly and record it in your report. Never remove the goal or a block whose `\lean{}` still names a live Lean declaration.
297	
298	Keep `leandag` edits inside your assigned chapter — fixing a dependency that lives in another chapter is a "Notes for Plan Agent" item, not a cross-chapter edit.

181	  unit sheaf-as-module \(\struct{U_i}\) (Mathlib's \(\mathtt{SheafOfModules.unit}\))
182	  carries a canonical free presentation on one generator with no relations;
183	  this presentation is finite.

185	
186	\begin{proof}
187	  \uses{lem:lbc_trivializing_cover}
188	  \leanok
189	  The structure sheaf-as-module \(\struct{U_i} = \mathtt{SheafOfModules.unit}\,
190	  (U_i).\mathtt{ringCatSheaf}\) is free of rank one on the single generator
191	  \(1\): the identity map \(\struct{U_i} \to \struct{U_i}\) exhibits it as
192	  \(\bigoplus_{i \in \{*\}} \struct{U_i}\), so the surjection
193	  \(\struct{U_i}^{\oplus 1} \twoheadrightarrow \struct{U_i}\) is an isomorphism
194	  and its kernel is zero, generated by the empty family. Thus \(\struct{U_i}\)
195	  has the canonical presentation with \(n = 1\) generators and \(m = 0\)
196	  relations; this canonical \(\mathtt{unit}\)-presentation is the rank-one free
197	  presentation, and Mathlib records it as \emph{finite} (the
198	  \(\mathtt{SheafOfModules.unit}\) finite-presentation instance).
199	
200	  The chart presentation is obtained by transporting the canonical finite
201	  presentation of \(\mathtt{unit}\,(U_i).\mathtt{ringCatSheaf}\) along the
202	  isomorphism \(e_i\), using \(\mathtt{SheafOfModules.Presentation.ofIsIso}\).
203	  This construction carries any finite presentation of a module to a finite
204	  presentation of any isomorphic module, so the transported presentation of
205	  \(M|_{U_i}\) is again finite.
206	  % NOTE. The over-restrict categorical bridge is the shared construction
207	  % `Scheme.Modules.chartOverIso` (lem:chart_over_iso,
208	  % chap:Picard_SheafOverEquivalence).
209	\end{proof}

212	\label{sec:lbc_finite_presentation}

215	[Locally trivial \(\Rightarrow\) finitely presented]
216	  \label{thm:lbc_isFinitePresentation}
217	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.LineBundle.IsLocallyTrivial.isFinitePresentation}
218	  \uses{lem:lbc_trivializing_cover, lem:lbc_chart_presentation, rem:lbc_site_instances}
219	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Modules on Ringed Spaces,
220	  % Lemma 17.25.4 (Tag 0B8M), invertible vs locally free of rank one
221	  % (read from references/stacks-modules.tex, L4159-L4165).
222	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
223	  % "Let $(X, \mathcal{O}_X)$ be a ringed space. Any locally free
224	  %  $\mathcal{O}_X$-module of rank $1$ is invertible.
225	  %  If all stalks $\mathcal{O}_{X, x}$ are local rings, then
226	  %  the converse holds as well (but in general this is not the case)."
227	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], Tag~0B8M (Modules, Lemma~17.25.4).}
228	  Let \(X\) be a scheme satisfying \cref{rem:lbc_site_instances} and let
229	  \(M : X.\mathtt{Modules}\) be locally trivial, \(\texttt{IsLocallyTrivial}(M)\). Then
230	  \(M\) is finitely presented: \(M.\mathtt{IsFinitePresentation}\) holds in
231	  Mathlib's \(\mathtt{SheafOfModules.IsFinitePresentation}\).
232	\end{theorem}
233	
234	\begin{proof}
235	  \uses{lem:lbc_trivializing_cover, lem:lbc_chart_presentation}
236	  \leanok
237	  By \cref{lem:lbc_trivializing_cover} choose a trivialising affine cover
238	  \(\{U_i\}_{i \in I}\) with isomorphisms \(e_i : M|_{U_i} \cong \struct{U_i}\).
239	  Assemble these into a \(\mathtt{QuasicoherentData}\) for \(M\): the index type
240	  is \(I\), the covering objects are the affine opens \(U_i\) (which cover \(X\)),
241	  and the presentation attached to index \(i\) is the finite free presentation of
242	  \(M|_{U_i}\) (equivalently, of the restriction \(M.\mathtt{over}\,U_i\))
243	  produced in \cref{lem:lbc_chart_presentation}. Each such presentation is
244	  finite by \cref{lem:lbc_chart_presentation}; the assembled
245	  \(\mathtt{QuasicoherentData}\) therefore satisfies
246	  \(\mathtt{QuasicoherentData.IsFinitePresentation}\), and the constructor
247	  \(\mathtt{SheafOfModules.IsFinitePresentation.mk}\) yields
248	  \(M.\mathtt{IsFinitePresentation}\).
249	
250	  The only non-formal input is the trivialising cover; everything else is the
251	  definitional unwinding of finite presentation as a local property
252	  (Stacks, ``Modules of finite presentation''), since a rank-one free module is its own finite
253	  presentation. No stalk computation and no neighbourhood-spreading argument is
254	  used: the hypothesis already provides the local free models.
255	\end{proof}
